title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption:
Coverage of essential health services

Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.

The 2023 world map displays the coverage of essential health services using a color gradient where darker blue indicates higher index scores and lighter green indicates lower scores. North America, Western Europe, Australia, and most of South America are shaded in the darkest blue, placing them in the 80 to 100 band of the index. Eastern Europe and parts of East Asia generally fall within the 60 to 80 range. A broad band of medium-to-light blue covers much of Latin America, Russia, and China, corresponding to values roughly between 40 and 60. The lowest values on the map are concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia, where countries appear in pale green shades representing the 0 to 20 and 20 to 40 bands. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data is available for that region. The index assesses coverage based on risk-standardized death rates and the implementation of common interventions. This measurement framework acknowledges that countries provide a wide range of services for health promotion, prevention, treatment, and care, including rehabilitation and palliation, utilizing tracer indicators to represent overall coverage of essential services. The visual distribution shows a distinct geographic pattern where high-income regions cluster at the top of the scale while specific regions in Africa and Asia occupy the lower bands.
